coredata.dc:title: Zwitterionic carbon quantum dots incorporated ultrafiltration membrane for efficient removal of copper ion
coredata.dc:creator: Yang, Hong-Li
Huang, Ching-Ting
Lin, Hsin-Yi
Chen, Yueh-Hung
Tsai, Hui-An
Lee, Kueir-Rarn
coredata.dc:description: Copper, prized for its excellent thermal and electrical conductivity, poses risks when present in wastewater. Treating copper ion-containing wastewater is vital for human and environmental safety. Membrane separation technology offers an eco-friendly and energy-efficient solution. In this study, a mixture of cellulose acetate (CA) and thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) was used to fabricate the tubular membrane, with quantum dots (QDs) added. Three types of QDs were introduced into the tubular membrane: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), tertiary amine CQDs (TQDs), and zwitterionic CQDs (ZQDs). At pH = 10, copper ions were chelated with sodium dimethyldithiocarbamate (SDDC) at a ratio of SDDC/Cu2+=10 (w/w), signifying a molar concentration (mol.L-1) ratio of SDDC:Cu2+=3:16. This process achieved a 99.8% removal rate of copper ion. The ZQDs-M exhibited high pure water flux of 6,227.4 L.m−2.h−1.bar−1 and 95.4% copper ion-complex rejection rate. The ZQDs-M also demonstrated excellent anti-adhesion properties, with only 4.55% relative fluorescence intensity of E. coli and 34.58% BSA adsorption. The BSA dynamic anti-fouling property of the ZQDs-M was also superior to that of tubular membrane without QDs addition.

head.ce:title: Zwitterionic carbon quantum dots incorporated ultrafiltration membrane for efficient removal of copper ion

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: R&D Center for Membrane Technology and Department of Chemical Engineering, Chung Yuan Christian University, Taoyuan 32023, Taiwan

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Copper, prized for its excellent thermal and electrical conductivity, poses risks when present in wastewater. Treating copper ion-containing wastewater is vital for human and environmental safety. Membrane separation technology offers an eco-friendly and energy-efficient solution. In this study, a mixture of cellulose acetate (CA) and thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) was used to fabricate the tubular membrane, with quantum dots (QDs) added. Three types of QDs were introduced into the tubular membrane: carbon quantum dots (CQDs), tertiary amine CQDs (TQDs), and zwitterionic CQDs (ZQDs). At pH = 10, copper ions were chelated with sodium dimethyldithiocarbamate (SDDC) at a ratio of SDDC/Cu2+=10 (w/w), signifying a molar concentration (mol.L-1) ratio of SDDC:Cu2+=3:16. This process achieved a 99.8% removal rate of copper ion. The ZQDs-M exhibited high pure water flux of 6,227.4 L.m−2.h−1.bar−1 and 95.4% copper ion-complex rejection rate. The ZQDs-M also demonstrated excellent anti-adhesion properties, with only 4.55% relative fluorescence intensity of E. coli and 34.58% BSA adsorption. The BSA dynamic anti-fouling property of the ZQDs-M was also superior to that of tubular membrane without QDs addition.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Wastewater containing metal ions should be treated before being discharged into the water stream. This is a significant issue in many industries, including electroplating, batteries, metal smelters, leather tanning and finishing, steel power plants, and organic chemistry [1–3]. This is because humans can be vulnerable to even low concentrations of metal ions, which can lead to poisoning or liver damage. Therefore, the main focus of this work is the removal of copper ions due to its significance [4] and its common use in industrial applications. According to the World Health Organization (WHO), the permissible limit of copper ions in drinking water is 1 ppm [5]. The United States Environmental Protection Agency (USEPA) has set the tolerable limit of copper ions in drinking water at 1.3 ppm. Meanwhile, the quality limit for chemical industry wastewater discharge, as formulated by Taiwan Environmental Protection Administration, sets the concentration of copper ions at 1.5 ppm. Consequently, numerous researchers made significant efforts to develop various solutions to address this issue. Examples include improving ion removal performance [6], reducing process costs [1], and minimizing sludge volume [7].

Currently, various technologies, including ion exchange, adsorption, and membrane separation, have been applied to the treatment of metal ion-containing wastewater [6]. These technologies come with distinct advantages and disadvantages, and the choice of which one to use depends on the specific requirements of the company or researcher [7]. In general, when considering wastewater treatment options, factors such as efficiency, reliability, environmental impact, and cost must all be taken into account. Membrane separation technology stands out as a suitable choice for treating wastewater containing metal ions due to its advantages, including low energy consumption [8], simple operation process [9], and high rejection efficiency [10]. Nanofiltration [11,12] and ultrafiltration [13,14] are membrane separation technologies that can effectively treat wastewater containing metal ions. The nanofiltration process aims to separate multivalent ions from monovalent ions though mechanisms such as the sieving effect [12] or the Donnan effect. The nanofiltration process allows for the concentration of valuable metal ions. This means that not only can the issue of metal ion-containing wastewater be addressed, but it also provides a cost-effective means of obtaining valuable metal ions. On another hand, the ultrafiltration membrane has pore size ranging from 1 to 100 nm [15], which are larger than the size of ions. As a result, adsorptive ultrafiltration membrane [13] and soluble polymer-assisted ultrafiltration membrane [14] have been integrated into the ultrafiltration process to enhance separation performance. These membranes can capture the desired metal ions either through adsorption or chelation mechanisms. In the case of chelating ultrafiltration, copper ions can be effectively captured by sodium dimethyl dithiocarbamate (SDDC) through a chelation mechanism. The solution containing the copper ion complex is typically turbid, requiring an extended period of time (e.g., 24 h) to allow for the separation of clear water in the upper layer. However, with the application of membrane ultrafiltration separation technology, clean water can be obtained without the need for a lengthy waiting period.

Cellulose acetate (CA) polymers are known for their hydrophilic nature and cost-effectiveness, but they exhibit limited alkaline stability [16]. On the other hand, thermoplastic polyurethane (TPU) is valued for its thermal and chemical stability [17], although it is inherently hydrophobic [18]. When used as polymers to prepare ultrafiltration membranes, blending CA with TPU allows for a complementary combination of properties, balancing hydrophilicity and chemical stability.

Carbon quantum dots (CQDs) are a type of zero-dimensional carbon-based material. Due to their hydrophilic and photocatalytic properties, CQDs garnered significant attention from researchers in the realms of water purification and membrane separation [19,20]. Nonetheless, when compared to various other membrane separation procedures, the utilization of CQDs in ultrafiltration processes might still be considered unexplored [19]. In our previous study we observed that the addition of CQDs led to an enhancement in membrane permeability [21].

Membrane fouling poses a significant challenge in the ultrafiltration process, as it has the potential to diminish membrane performance and lifetime. Zwitterionic modification is a frequently employed technique for mitigating this challenge in membrane separation processes. Due to their strong attraction to water molecules, zwitterions can bolster membrane hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of the membrane through electrostatic interactions [22,23]. Numerous methods, including surface grafting, surface segregation, biomimetic adhesion and surface coating, can be employed to incorporate zwitterionic molecules into membranes for surface zwitterionic modification [23].

Despite existing literature reports on zwitterionic modification of QDs [24–27], its application in the field of membranes has not been widely explored. This study focuses on augmenting the hydrophilicity and antifouling properties of tubular membranes through the zwitterionic modification of CQDs. Modified CQDs was introduced into the CA/TPU blended dope to fabricate tubular membrane via the wet phase inversion method. Subsequently, these membranes were employed in a chelating ultrafiltration process for the treatment of wastewater containing copper ions.

CA polymer (CA-394-60S) was acquired from Eastman Chemical Company (Palo Alto, CA, USA). TPU (Utechllan® UT7-95AU10) was provided by Taiwan Covestro Co., Ltd. Reagent grade N-methyl-2-pyrrolidone (NMP), procured from Tedia Company Inc. (Fairfield, OH, USA), served as a solvent for the preparation of the CA/TPU blended casting solution. Polyester tubular substrate and sodium dimethyl dithiocarbamate (SDDC) were obtained from King Membrane Energy Technology Inc., Taiwan. Anhydrous citric acid, copper sulfate (CuSO4), and sodium hydroxide (NaOH) were procured from Showa Chemical Co., Ltd. (Tokyo, Japan). The zwitterionic modifiers of CQDs, 1-ethyl-3-(3-(dimethylamino) propyl)-carbodiimide (EDC) and 1, 4-butanesultone (1,4-BS), were purchased from Alfa Aesar Co., Inc. Ethanol and glycerol were sourced from Echo Chemical Co., Ltd. Bovine serum albumin (BSA) and phosphate buffered saline (PBS) were purchased from Uniregion Bio-Tech Inc. and Sigma-Aldrich Co., Ltd., respectively. Dual-RangeTM Bradford Reagent (Visual Protein BR01-500) was purchased from Energenesis Biomedical Co., Ltd. Lysogeny broth (LB), 2-amino-2-methyl-1-propanol (AMP), isopropyl β-D-1-thiogalactopyranoside (IPTG) and glutaraldehyde required for antibacterial tests were purchased from Bio Future, PanReac AppliChem Inc., Bionovas Biotechnology Co., Ltd. and Fisher Scientific Co., LLC.

The synthesis process of CQDs remained consistent with our previous work [21]. Citric acid underwent pyrolysis at 210 ℃ for 3 hr. Following this, dialysis and filtration were carried out to maintain the desired size of the CQDs. Subsequently, the CQDs were subjected to drying through a rotary evaporator and freeze dryer, resulting in the production of dried CQDs powders.

Fig. 1 illustrates a schematic diagram of CQDs modification. The modification process began with the conversion of CQDs into TQDs using EDC, following a procedure outlined in the literature [28]. Subsequently, these TQDs were further modified through zwitterionization using 1,4-BS to produce ZQDs. The process involved adding 1 g of CQDs to a 2 mL PBS aqueous solution containing 0.23 g of EDC. This mixture was then dilute to 20 mL using distilled water and allowed to react at pH = 4 for 4 hr. After this, dialysis was performed using a 1,000 Da dialysis bag for 30 min, followed by freeze-drying to obtain TQDs containing tertiary amine groups.

The zwitterionization modification process for ZQDs followed the same procedure outlined in the literature as that for TQDs modification [28]. Specifically, 0.08 g of 1,4-BS and 0.5 g of TQDs were introduced into a 50 mL aqueous solution containing 50 v/v% ethanol. The mixture was allowed to react at room temperature for a duration of 11 hr. Subsequently, the solution underwent dialysis using a 1,000 Da dialysis bag for 30 min, followed by a 24 hr freeze-drying process to yield ZQDs.

The composition of the polymer solution used to create tubular membranes is detailed in Table 1 . Initially, various QDs are added to NMP and homogenize at 12,000 rpm for 30 min. Following this step, CA and TPU are introduced with a combined polymer concentration of 13 wt% and a CA to TPU ratio of 8:2 into the previously prepared QDs/NMP solution. The resulting CA/TPU + QDs dope solution was then achieved through stirring at 50℃ until complete dissolution of the polymer was achieved.

The membranes were manufactured using a non-solvent induced phase separation at room temperature. To achieve this, a self-assembled tubular membrane fabrication frame was utilized, and the dissolved casting solution was coated onto the outer surface of a polyester substrate tube using a spinneret with a diameter of 2.3 mm. Following this application, the cast tubular film was passed through an air gap of 15 cm and introduced into a coagulation bath (water). This process was conducted at a take-up speed of 2.4 cm/s, completing the phase inversion process. Additionally, the dissolved casting solution was also cast on a glass plate using a 300 µm casting knife. The resulting cast film was then immersed in water bath to prepare a free-standing membrane, which was used for characterizing properties such as water contact angle (WCA), E. coli adsorption, and BSA adsorption of the prepared membrane. A diagram depicting the module preparation for the tubular membrane test is presented in Figure S1 .

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of QDs and prepared membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The elemental composition and functional groups of the QDs and prepared membranes were analyzed using XPS (Model: Thermo Scientific, K-Alpha) and FTIR (LE186-0078, Perkin Elmer). To define the morphology and size of the QDs, transmission electron microscopy (TEM, JEOL JEM-2100, Japan) was employed. For observing the surface and cross-sectional morphology of the membranes, field emission scanning electron microscopy (FESEM, S-4800, Hitachi Co, Japan) was utilized. Energy-dispersive X-ray spectroscopy (EDX) was performed using SEM. ImageJ software was used for analyzing the pore size and surface porosity of the membrane surface. To assess the membrane surface roughness in terms of root mean square (Rq), atomic force microscope (AFM, Bruker Dimension Icon., USA) was employed. The hydrophilicity and wettability were measured using the water contact angle (WCA) (PD-VP Type, FACE Automatic Interfacial Tensiometer,). The zeta potential was analyzed using surface potential analyzer for solid sample (SurPASS, SurPASS 3, Anton Paar Austria GmbH, Austria) in a 0.01 M KCl solution at pH 7.

The error bars for pore size, surface porosity, skin layer thickness, surface roughness, and zeta potential represent the standard deviation of the membrane samples. Each average and standard deviation were calculated based on a minimum of 4 membrane samples.

The filtration performance was assessed through pure water flux, chelation tests, and UF performance. The error bars of pure water flux and chelating complex rejection rate (in UF performance) represent the standard deviation of the membrane samples. Each average and standard deviation were calculated based on a minimum of 4 membrane samples. However, the error bars of copper ion removal in chelation tests represent the feed solution samples after 4 ppm copper ions were chelated with 40 ppm SDDC at pH 10. The feed solution samples were repeated at least 4 times.

The setup for vacuum-assisted filtration is illustrated in Figure S2 , and the operating pressure is 0.1 bar. The pure water flux was calculated using equation (1) below:

where V, A and Δt represent permeate volume (L), membrane effective area (m2), and sampling time (hr), respectively.

A 400 ppm copper ion solution was prepared by dissolving 0.8 g of CuSO4 in DI water. Then, the 400 ppm copper ion solution was diluted to obtain a solution with a copper ion concentration of 4 ppm. The 4 ppm copper ion solution was then adjusted to pH = 10 using NaOH. Different amounts of SDDC (0, 20, 40, 60 ppm) were added to the alkaline solution containing copper ions at pH = 10 to serve as a chelating agent. The copper ion concentration before and after SDDC chelation was measured using ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–VIS, Model: DR6000 UV–VIS Benchtop Spectrophotometer, HACH). And copper ion removal was calculated using the following equation (2):

where Ccu0 and Ccu1 represent the concentration of copper ions (ppm) before and after SDDC chelation, respectively.

The setup involves connecting the membrane module to the peristaltic pump. A 4 ppm Cu2+solution, chelated with 40 ppm SDDC at pH = 10, was used as the feed and then separated using the tubular membrane module at a peristaltic pump flow rate of 10 mL/min. The particle size of the SDDC chelated copper ion complex was assessed using dynamic light scattering (DLS, Zeta Nano ZS90, Malvern Zetasizer), as depicted in Figure S3 . The membrane functioned to retain the copper ion complex. A ProQuatro meter (Model: Multiparameter Meter, YSI Inc.) was used to determine the concentration of copper ion complex in the feed and permeate during test. The chelating complex rejection rate was calculated using equation (3).

ce:section.ce:section-title: BSA static adsorption and BSA dynamic filtration tests
ce:section.ce:para:
For the BSA static adsorption test, the membrane was soaked in 0.1 M PBS for 24 hr, and then transferred to a 1,000 ppm BSA solution for 3 hr. The BSA solution concentration was then analyzed by ultraviolet–visible spectroscopy (UV–VIS, PowerWave HT, BioTek Instruments, Inc.) using the Bradford protein assay method. The BSA static adsorption was calculated according to equation (4):
(4)

where V, Cb , Ca , and A were volume of BSA solution (L), the initial concentration of BSA (ppm), the BSA concentration after adsorption (ppm), and membrane effective area (cm2), respectively.

For the BSA dynamic filtration test, the membrane was initially stabilized with DI water for 1 hr, during which the pure water flux (Jw,i ) was sampled every 5 min. Subsequently, the feed was replaced with a 1,000 ppm BSA solution, and the flux (Jp,i ) was sampled every 5 min. Following this, the membrane underwent a 5 min backwash with DI water. This filtration test was repeated for 3 cycles. After each cycle of the BSA filtration test, the membrane was backwashed with DI water at a peristaltic pump rate of 5 mL/min for 5 min. The BSA dynamic filtration test results were analyzed as calculated in equation (5)–(8).

500 µL AMP was added to a tissue culture polystyrene (TCPS) plate containing 12.5 g of LB and 500 mL of DI water at 37 ℃. E. coli was cultured in the above solution at 37 ℃ in a thermostatic shaker at 100 rpm for 12 hr. To endow green fluorescence to E. coli, 240 µL of IPTG aqueous solution was added. The membrane was immersed in the E. coli solution at 37 ℃ in a thermostatic shaker at 100 rpm for 24 hr, and the E. coli solution was changed every 6 hr. Afterward, a 2.5 v/v% GA aqueous solution replaced the E. coli solution and reacted with the E. coli on the membrane surface at 4 ℃ for 4 hr to fix the E. coli. Subsequently, the membrane was washed three times with PBS.

The bacteria attachment was observed using Confocal laser scanning microscopy (CLSM, Model: A1R+, Laser module LU-N4, Nikon Co., Tokyo, Japan). The E. coli adsorption ratio was calculated using the following equation:

The membrane lifetime for ZQDs-M was determined by conducting separate pure water flux and UF performance tests at pH = 10. The feed solution consisted of 4 ppm Cu2+ +40 ppm SDDC. Pure water flux was plotted directly as the volume of permeate over time, while the copper complex rejection rate was determined using the same method as described in section 2.5.2.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Characterization of CQDs and modified QDs
ce:section.ce:para:
The morphology of the synthesized CQDs and modified QDs (TQDs and ZQDs) was observed using TEM, as illustrated in Fig. 2
(a-c). The modified QDs exhibited morphology similar to pristine CQDs reported in the literature [29], with a mean diameter of less than 10 nm. Specifically, the mean diameters of CQDs, TQDs, and ZQDs were determined to be 3.08 ± 1.32, 3.08 ± 1.08, and 3.09 ± 1.10 nm, respectively, as depicted in Fig. 2 (d).

The FTIR spectra shown in Fig. 2 (e) provided insights into the functional groups present in the synthesized and modified QDs. In the synthesized CQDs, vibrations of C-O, C-O-C, C-OH, COO–, C = O and stretching of OH were observed at 1068, 1280, 1391, 1553, 1651, and 3321 cm−1, respectively. When EDC reacted with CQDs to form TQDs, there was a decrease in the intensity of the hydroxyl peaks at 1391 and 3312 cm−1, and new peaks emerged at 1166, 1482, and 2971 cm−1. These new peaks corresponded to the C-N stretching of tertiary amine from N-acylurea [30], scissoring of CH2 [31], and stretching of CH2. The zwitterionic functional group was easily detected after 1, 4-BS reacted with the N-acylurea, resulting in peaks at 912, 1040 and 1172, and 2875 cm−1 in the ZQDs. These peaks were associated with the C-N stretching of quaternary amine [32], asymmetry and symmetry stretching of SO3
-
[33], and C–H stretching of N+(CH3)3.

The XPS narrow scan N1 spectrum and wide scan analysis of the CQDs, TQDs and ZQDs were shown in Fig. 2 (f-h) and the composition of QDs is presented in Table S1 . It can be found that after modification of CQDs, a small nitrogen peak can be observed in TQDs, while a sulfur peak can be observed in ZQDs. This is to be expected since CQDs was modified.

From the nitrogen narrow scan of TQDs (Fig. 2 (g)), it can be observed that the peaks at 398.9 and 401.2 eV correspond to C-N and –NH-CO bonds [28], respectively, indicating the successful reaction of EDC with CQDs. In Fig. 2 (h), the C-N, N–H, and C-N+ [34] peaks were observed at 399.0, 401.2, and 401.8 eV, respectively. The appearance of the new peak at C-N+ indicates the successful reaction of 1, 4-BS with EDC. Moreover, the area percentage of C-N+ peaks increased to 17.0%, which is similar to that of the –NH-CO peaks (16.2%). The area percentage of tertiary amine (C-N) decreased from 69.9% to 66.8%.

The zwitterionic degree can be determined using the following equation (10) [35]:

The transfer of tertiary amine (C-N bonding) to zwitterionic molecules (quaternary amine, C-N+ bonding) was quantified as the zwitterionic degree, which was found to be 20.3% for ZQDs.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Morphology and characterization of the hybrid membranes
ce:section.ce:para: The morphology of the prepared hybrid membranes is depicted in Fig. 3 . SEM observations of the membrane surface (Fig. 3 (A1-D1)) reveal the presence of porous membranes, obtained to the incorporation of hydrophilic QDs into the dope solution. This inclusion of hydrophilic QDs might potentially leads to an increase in the non-solvent inflow rate, resulting in a porous membrane surface. Furthermore, the introduction of hydrophilic QDs into the doping solution is associated with an increased non-solvent inflow rate, which is responsible for the formation of macrovoids in all the prepared membranes, as evidenced in the cross-section images (Fig. 3 (A3-D3)).

The surface pore size, surface porosity, and skin layer thickness of the prepared membranes are presented in Table 2 . With the incorporation of QDs, the membrane exhibited an increase in pore size and surface porosity. Especially, the surface porosity of the membrane followed this sequence: ZQDs-M > TQDs-M > CQDs-M > Pristine-M. Conversely, the skin layer thickness of the prepared membrane followed the opposite sequence, namely Pristine-M > CQDs-M > TQDs-M > ZQDs-M. These findings suggest that the addition of QDs led to the development of more porous membranes compared to the QDs addition-free membrane.

In the case of membranes containing QDs, TQDs possess a higher number of hydrophilic amine group compared to CQDs. Consequently, the non-solvent inflow during membrane formation was influenced by TQDs, leading to TQDs-M being more porous than CQDs-M. Following the zwitterionization process, ZQDs might undergo ionic solvation and form strong electrostatic interactions with the water molecules [22]. Moreover, owing to the highly hydrophilic nature of ZQDs, the most porous membrane, ZQDs-M, was obtained.

The surface roughness and zeta potential are also provided in Table 3 . The sequence of surface roughness is as follows: ZQDs-M > TQDs-M > CQDs-M > Pristine-M, consistent with the trend observed for surface porosity (Table 2). This correlation arises from the fact that surface roughness is closely associated with surface pores. Consequently, a membrane with a greater surface roughness signifies a larger size or a greater number of surface pores.

It can be observed that the Pristine-M has a negative charge. This negative charge may be originated from the inherent negative charge formed by the functional groups of CA polymer, such as hydroxyl groups. After adding QDs, the Zeta potential of the membrane ranged from 15.90 to −18.09 mV. Notably, these changes all fall within the standard deviation, which is likely because of the minimal addition of QDs (1,000 ppm), resulting in minimal changes in the membrane zeta potential.

As observed in the water contact angle of the membranes in Fig. 4
(a), the water contact angle of all membranes decreased over time. This is due to the fact that the hydrophilic porous membrane easily adsorbs water molecules. Meanwhile, ZQDs-M exhibited the lowest water contact angle and the fastest decline rate. This is attributed to ZQDs-M having the highest membrane surface roughness, as well as the zwitterionic functional groups having a strong affinity for water molecules, allowing them to adsorb more water into the membrane.

However, TQDs-M showed similar hydrophilicity to CQDs-M. Although ternary amine was introduced into TQDs, carboxylic acids were consumed during the amination process. Therefore, the hydrophilicity of the TQDs-M was only slightly improved compared to CQDs-M.

From the FTIR spectrum in Fig. 4 (b), the pure CA membrane exhibited characteristic peaks at 1041, 1230, and 1744 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of C-O-C, C-O, and C = O, and a peak at 1369 cm−1, corresponding to the bending of C–H. The pure TPU showed characteristic peak at 771, 1222, 1529, 1702, 2959, and 3334 cm−1, corresponding to the stretching vibration of C–H, C–C, C = C, C = O, C–H, and N–H. Pristine-M exhibited the characteristic peaks of CA and TPU, and there was no discernible difference between it and all QDs hybrid membranes, which might be due to the small addition of QDs.

XPS was employed to further characterize the elements present in both the Pristine-M and QDs-added hybrid membranes, as depicted in Fig. 4 (c). Sulfur was only observed in the ZQDs-M sample, which could be attributed to the zwitterionic functional group present in the ZQDs. Additionally, the EDX results confirmed our prediction, revealing the presence of sulfur on the surface of the ZQDs-M membrane, as illustrated in Fig. S4(b). Notably, the cross-sectional EDX image displayed a uniform distribution of sulfur, as shown in Fig. S4(d).

The element analysis results of Pristine-M and QDs-added hybrid membranes were tabulated in Table 4 . The N element detected in Pristine-M corresponded to the ternary amine (N-acylurea) present in TPU. The oxygen atomic percentage of CQDs-M increased from 32.63 (Pristine-M) to 34.47 at% (CQDs-M) after the addition of CQDs, owing to the abundant oxygen functional groups of CQDs. Therefore, both the C and N atomic percentages decreased with the addition of CQDs. The nitrogen atomic percent of TQDs-M slightly increased after amination, with the nitrogen element corresponding to the N-acylurea of TQDs. Concurrently, the oxygen atomic percentage decreased due to the consumption of carboxylic acid and hydroxyl functional groups of CQDs during the reaction. After zwitterionization, a sulfur element of 0.21 at% was detected in ZQDs-M, confirming the successful zwitterionization. Additionally, the oxygen element increased from 31.44 (TQDs-M) to 33.64 at% (ZQDs-M) due to the presence of oxygen functional groups from sulfonic acid.
The copper ions chelation removal rate and separation performance of the QDs-added membranes, including pure water flux and chelating complex rejection rate, were presented in Fig. 5 .

Different amounts of SDDC were added to an alkaline solution containing copper ions at pH = 10 [36], based on the data from Table S2 . Table S2 illustrates the influence of pH values on the copper ion concentration in the feed solution. pH values significantly affect the concentration of copper ions in the solution, with the solution readily adopting a chelated form beyond pH = 6 [37]. Fig. 5 (a) illustrates the effect of chelation with varying concentrations of SDDC on the removal of copper ions. It can be observed from the figure that when the copper ion solution is adjusted to pH = 10, the removal rate of copper ions without SDDC chelation is approximately 69.1%. With the addition of SDDC to chelate copper ions, the removal rate of copper ions increases as the SDDC concentration rises. When the SDDC concentration was 40 ppm, the removal of copper ions reached 99.8%, indicating that most of the copper ions in the feed solution were converted to copper complexes. This was more efficient than the 97.9% removal observed when using 20 ppm SDDC concentration. Although with a 60 ppm SDDC concentration, the removal rate of copper ions is higher than that with a 40 ppm SDDC concentration, reaching 99.9%, it's important to note that at a 40 ppm SDDC concentration, the copper ion concentration is only 0.008 ppm, significantly lower than the Taiwanese government's requirements for copper ion concentration in wastewater (1.5 ppm) and drinking water (1 ppm). Moreover, the limit set by the World Health Organization for the disposal of copper ions to prevent human poisoning is 0.02 ppm, which is also much higher than the value (0.008 ppm) found in this study. Therefore, based on SDDC cost considerations associated with SDDC, the concentration of 40 ppm was selected as the optimal addition amount. Table S3 demonstrates that even at various copper ion concentrations (2 ∼ 10 ppm Cu2+), the addition of 40 ppm of SDDC continues to exhibit excellent copper ion removal rates. In this study, the choice of using a low-concentration copper ion solution as the feed is primarily because the electrical and semiconductor industry frequently generates low-concentration copper ion wastewater solutions [38].

After chelation of the copper ion complex solution with SDDC, even when the copper ion concentration is as low as 0.008 ppm, the complex causes the solution to become turbid. If one intends to precipitate the copper ion complex to obtain a clear solution in the upper layer, it needs to stand for at least 24 h. Furthermore, the copper ion complex precipitate in the lower layer, after precipitation, also needs to undergo concentration due to the water content. If the solution goes through the ultrafiltration separation procedure using the membrane, the SDDC chelated copper ion complex solution can rapidly remove water from the copper ion complex solution without requiring a standing procedure.

Fig. 5 (b) displays the pure water flux and the UF performance in terms of the complex rejection rate for both the Pristine-M and the QDs-added hybrid membranes. As evident from the figure, the pure water flux of the membranes follows the order ZQDs-M > TQDs-M > CQDs-M > Pristine-M, while maintaining a copper complex rejection rate above 94.2%. This trend is consistent with the surface porosity and roughness data presented in Table 2 and Table 3. Higher porosity and greater roughness correspond to increased pure water flux. Additionally, the skin thickness detailed in Table 2 and the WCA shown in Fig. 4 (a) indicate that thinner skin thickness and lower WCA translate to reduced mass transfer resistance and heightened membrane hydrophilicity, respectively. These findings further substantiate the observed trend in pure water flux.

During wastewater treatment, bacteria in wastewater can adhere to material surfaces through various forces, including Coulomb force, Van der Waals force, and H-bond. In some cases, this adhesion can become irreversible, leading to biofouling. Hence, it's essential to develop effective strategies to disrupt the interaction between bacteria and material surfaces. The anti-adhesion property of the prepared membranes were assessed through bacterial attachment and protein adsorption tests [39], as presented in Fig. 6
,
Figure S6
and Table 5 , respectively. Lower E. coli attachment and BSA adsorption indicate superior anti-bacterial and anti-protein adsorption properties. From the observation in Fig. 6 and Table 5, it's evident that the pristine membrane without QDs has the highest relative fluorescence intensity of E. coli and BSA adsorption. Upon adding QDs, both E. coli relative fluorescence intensity of and the BSA adsorption decrease. However, membranes with TQDs additions exhibit higher E. coli relative fluorescence intensity and BSA adsorption compared to those with unmodified CQDs and modified ZQDs. This phenomenon might be due to the fact that the adsorption property is affected by both physical (roughness) and chemical (hydrophilicity) properties. Higher surface roughness can make it easier for bacteria and proteins to adhere to the membrane surface, leading to increased attachment of bacteria and adsorption of proteins. Conversely, higher hydrophilicity can prevent bacteria or proteins from adhering to the membrane surface, resulting in improved anti-adhesion performance. Consequently, all QDs-add hybrid membranes demonstrate enhanced anti-adhesion properties compared to the Pristine-M, primarily due to their increased hydrophilicity. Although the TQDs-M exhibits similar dynamic contact angle behavior to the CQDs-M (as shown in Fig. 4 (a)), its surface roughness is greater than that of the CQDs-M. Consequently, the anti-adhesion properties of TQDs-M are inferior to those of CQDs-M. On the other hand, despite having the highest surface roughness among the QD-added membranes, ZQDs-M displays the best anti-adhesion performance due to its highest hydrophilicity.

The anti-fouling property of both the Pristine-M and the ZQDs-M was evaluated through 3 cycles BSA dynamic filtration test, with the results are presented in Fig. 7
(a). After 420 min of this test, the normalized flux of Pristine-M and ZQDs-M decreased to 39.6% and 69.6%, respectively. In comparison to Pristine-M, ZQDs-M exhibited a lower decline in normalized flux, attributed to its superior hydrophilicity, anti-adhesion property, and the presence of a hydration layer. This hydration layer might be formed due to ionic solvation and strong electrostatic interactions between zwitterionic molecules in ZQDs-M and water molecules. As a consequence, it seems that hydrophobic BSA may have experienced reduced adhesion to the membrane, which could possibly make it easier to remove during backwashing, potentially resulting in enhanced anti-fouling properties.

Generally, when a membrane exhibits higher FRR and Rr along with lower Rir, it tends to have better BSA dynamic anti-fouling property. The results for the BSA dynamic anti-fouling properties after the first cycle were shown in Fig. 7 (b) and Table 6 . The FRR1 and Rr,1 increased from 73.3% (Pristine-M) to 88.8% (ZQDs-M) and 55.8% (Pristine-M) to 77.9% (ZQDs-M), respectively, while the Rir,1 decreased from 26.6% to 11.1%. These changes can be attributed to the enhanced hydrophilicity and anti-adhesion property of the membrane.

The FRRs for the three-cycle BSA dynamic filtration tests were depicted in Fig. 7 (c) and summarized in Table 6. Following 3 cycles of testing, the FRR for the ZQDs-M membrane remained consistently above 73.4 %, a significant improvement compared to Pristine-M (45.0 %). This enhancement might be attributed to the increased hydrophilicity and improved anti-adhesion property resulting from the addition of ZQDs-M. These findings further support the enhanced anti-fouling property associated with ZQDs.

The pure water flux through volume and the copper complex rejection rate of ZQDs-M over time are depicted in Fig. 8 . The lifetime of the membrane is crucial in both water flux and UF test [40]. The copper ion feed concentration was 4 ppm, and the test was conducted with 40 ppm of SDDC at pH = 10. From the figure, it can be observed that within a three-hour test, ZQDs-M achieved a pure water flux of 34.2 L, and even after three hours of testing, it still maintained around 93% of copper complex rejection rate, clearly meeting the emission standards Taiwan.

The performance comparison between this study and the literature is shown in Table 7 . The removal of copper ions reached 99.6%, confirming that the majority of the copper ions had indeed transformed into chelates. Therefore, the copper rejection rate was considered to be 95.4%, which is the same as the copper complex rejection rate. When compared to the literature, it is evident that ZQDs-M exhibits a commendable copper rejection rate. Although not the highest, it meets the standards perfectly, with the copper concentration at the permeate significantly below regulatory limits. Consequently, a comparison of ZQDs-M with other literature can be based on pure water flux. Observations from the comparison of pure water flux between ZQDs-M and other literature reveal that ZQDs-M boasts a high flux of 6227.4 LMHB, which is more than ten times higher than that of other literature. This remarkable performance positions ZQDs-M as a promising candidate for commercialization, warranting further consideration in comparison to the literature.

PVC: polyvinyl chloride, HMO/Sil: hydrous manganese oxide modified by silane, NFM: nanofiber membrane, PES: polyethersulfone, PPy: polypyrrole, PVDF: polyvinylidene fluoride, A-MWCNTs: 3-aminopropyltriethoxysilane-functionalized multi-walled carbon nanotubes, PANI: polyaniline, PSf: polysulfone, Fe3O4/SiO2-Met: metformine-modified silica coated Fe3O4, PAN: polyacrylonitrile, BSA: bovine serum albumin, PAA: polyacrylic acid, PEI: polyethylenimine, PVA: polyvinyl alcohol, CS: chitosan, TEOS: tetraethylorthosilicate, CMC: carboxymethyl cellulose.

In this study, we aimed to prepare QDs-incorporated asymmetric tubular membranes for application in chelation ultrafiltration procedures for treating solutions containing copper ions. The addition of QDs resulted in a more porous and rougher membrane surface. Especially, for the ZQDs-incorporated membrane (ZQDs-M), the surface pore size, porosity, and surface roughness increased from 14.4 nm, 0.12%, and 17.06 nm of the QDs-free membrane (Pristine-M) to 15.3 nm, 0.37%, and 24.52 nm, respectively. By using an SDDC/Cu2+ratio of 10 (w/w), which represents a molar concentration (mol.L-1) ratio of SDDC:Cu2+= 3:16, copper ions were effectively transformed into copper ion complexes, resulting in a remarkable removal rate of 99.8%. In comparison to conventional methods employing polymer flocculants/coagulants, this approach significantly reduced the amount of sludge generated. These copper ion complexes were then effectively removed by a tubular membrane incorporating zwitterionic-modified ZQDs (ZQDs-M), resulting in a rejection rate of 95.4%. ZQDs-M exhibited a pure water flux of 6277.4 LMHB. Furthermore, ZQDs-M demonstrated excellent anti-adhesion properties, with a relative fluorescence intensity of 4.55% for E. coli and an adsorption capacity of 34.58 μg/cm2 for BSA. ZQDs-M also displayed improved dynamic antifouling performance when subjected to BSA dynamic filtration testing. After three cycles of BSA dynamic filtration, the FRR of the pristine tubular membrane without QD addition decreased from 73.3% (FRR1) to 45.0% (FRR3), a decrease of 28.3%. In contrast, the FRR of ZQDs-M only decreased by 15.4% (from FRR1 = 88.8% to FRR3 = 73.4%).
